_note: ASSYRIA king-list island, extending the existing Assyrian succession chain (ids prefixed AS_) from Erishum I (c.1950 BC, pre-existing tail AS_ERISHUM) through the Old Assyrian period and the Shamshi-Adad I interlude, the Middle Assyrian empire (Ashur-uballit I onward), its decline, and the Neo-Assyrian empire down to the Fall of Nineveh in 612 BC to the Medes & Babylonians. Rendered as a SUCCESSION chain: each king is the single 'child' of the prior, with a '—' placeholder spouse, ordered by strictly decreasing date (earliest BC at top). This is dynastic succession, NOT a continuous bloodline — several transitions were usurpations. Where adjacent kings are genuinely father-son (Shalmaneser I, Tukulti-Ninurta I, Adad-nirari II, Shalmaneser III, and the Sargonid line Sennacherib→Esarhaddon→Ashurbanipal), the label notes 'son of'. Bridge nodes (AS_OLDBRIDGE, AS_MIDDECLINE1, AS_MIDDECLINE2) compress long gaps of minor/less-attested reigns and carry no crown. All historical; dates are standard scholarly regnal approximations (middle chronology). Sources: Wikipedia 'List of Assyrian kings' and individual king articles; Assyrian King List.

children.label:
Old Assyrian period & Shamshi-Adad I interlude
c.1900–1400 BC · the later city-rulers of Ashur, then the Amorite conqueror Shamshi-Adad I (c.1808 BC) who briefly forged a north-Mesopotamian kingdom, followed by Assyria's eclipse under Mitanni · historical (bridge)

👑 Ashur-uballit I
c.1363 BC · threw off Mitanni overlordship and founded the Middle Assyrian empire; corresponded with Egypt in the Amarna letters · historical

👑 Adad-nirari I
c.1305 BC · expanded Assyria westward, conquering the Mitanni heartland of Hanigalbat · historical

👑 Shalmaneser I
c.1273 BC · son of Adad-nirari I; crushed the last of Mitanni and campaigned into the Nairi lands · historical

👑 Tukulti-Ninurta I
c.1243 BC · son of Shalmaneser I; sacked Babylon and carried off its king, empire at its Middle Assyrian height · historical

Post-Tukulti-Ninurta lull
c.1200 BC · after Tukulti-Ninurta I's assassination Assyria contracted under a run of weaker kings before recovering · historical (bridge)

👑 Tiglath-Pileser I
c.1114 BC · restored Assyrian power, campaigning to the Mediterranean and the upper Euphrates · historical

Middle Assyrian decline
c.1050–950 BC · Aramean migrations pressed in and Assyria shrank to its heartland through a long dark age before the Neo-Assyrian revival · historical (bridge)

👑 Ashur-dan II
c.934 BC · began the Neo-Assyrian recovery, reconsolidating the homeland and its economy · historical

👑 Adad-nirari II
c.911 BC · son of Ashur-dan II; often counted the first true Neo-Assyrian king, securing the borders for expansion · historical

👑 Ashurnasirpal II
c.883 BC · ruthless empire-builder; moved the capital to Kalhu (Nimrud) with a legendary inaugural feast · historical

👑 Shalmaneser III
c.859 BC · son of Ashurnasirpal II; fought Ahab of Israel's coalition at Qarqar (853 BC) and later took tribute from Jehu of Israel, shown on the Black Obelisk · historical

👑 Adad-nirari III
c.811 BC · campaigned against Damascus and the Levant; reigned early under his mother, the regent Sammu-ramat (the later legend of Semiramis) · historical

👑 Tiglath-Pileser III
c.745 BC · the great conqueror who rebuilt the army and empire, annexing much of the Levant and taking the throne of Babylon; the Bible's Pul · historical

👑 Shalmaneser V
c.727 BC · besieged Samaria, capital of the northern kingdom of Israel; died before its fall · historical

👑 Sargon II
c.722 BC · seized the throne and completed the fall of Samaria, deporting the northern tribes of Israel into exile; founded the new capital Dur-Sharrukin · historical

👑 Sennacherib
c.705 BC · son of Sargon II; besieged Jerusalem under King Hezekiah (701 BC) but did not take it; destroyed Babylon and rebuilt Nineveh as his capital · historical

👑 Esarhaddon
c.681 BC · son of Sennacherib; rebuilt Babylon and conquered Egypt, bringing Assyria to its greatest extent · historical

👑 Ashurbanipal
c.668 BC · son of Esarhaddon; last great king, whose library at Nineveh preserved thousands of cuneiform tablets including the Epic of Gilgamesh · historical

👑 Ashur-etil-ilani
c.631 BC · son of Ashurbanipal; reigned as the empire began to fracture amid internal strife · historical

👑 Sinsharishkun
c.627 BC · faced the rising Medes & Babylonians; died in the sack of Nineveh · historical

👑 Ashur-uballit II
c.612 BC · last king of Assyria; after Nineveh fell to the Medes & Babylonians (Fall of Nineveh, 612 BC) he held out at Harran until c.609 BC, when the empire ended · historical
